title: CNN This Morning · 2026-06-10 · Maine Senate Race: Platner vs. Collins
summary: In the Maine Democratic primary, scandal-plagued Graham Platter defeated Governor Janet Mills with about 75% of the vote, and will challenge Republican Senator Susan Collins. Platter needs to win over Mills' supporters, while Collins still has an advantage among independent voters. Other news: US launches missile strikes on Iran; OpenAI files for IPO; Epstein files hearing; Colorado gubernatorial candidate Victor Marx's controversial remarks.

paragraphs.speaker: Audie Cornish

ALEXANDRIA OCASIO-CORTEZ (D-NY): If the choice on the ballot is between that and a senator who's voted to take health care away from millions of Americans, that's the situation that we have to weigh.

sentences.en: Democrats in Maine vote to stick with Graham Platner.
sentences.cn: Democrats in Maine voted to stick with Graham Platner.
sentences.structure: Simple sentence: subject (Democrats in Maine) + verb (vote) + infinitive phrase (to stick with Graham Platner) as object.
sentences.analysis: "Stick with" means "continue to support," a common phrase in news.

sentences.en: Could that cost the party a shot at flipping the Senate this fall?
sentences.cn: Will this cost the party a chance to flip the Senate this fall?
sentences.structure: Interrogative: modal verb (Could) + subject (that) + predicate (cost) + indirect object (the party) + direct object (a shot at flipping the Senate this fall).
sentences.analysis: cost sb. a shot at sth. means "to make someone lose the opportunity to do something"; flip the Senate means "to flip control of the Senate", a common expression in political news.

sentences.en: If the choice on the ballot is between that and a senator who's voted to take health care away from millions of Americans, that's the situation that we have to weigh.
sentences.cn: If the choice on the ballot is between that (referring to Plattner's controversial behavior) and a senator who would vote to take health care away from millions of Americans, that is the situation we have to weigh.
sentences.structure: Conditional clause (If...) + main clause (that's the situation) + relative clause (that we have to weigh).
sentences.analysis: weigh means "to consider carefully", an abstract verb; take health care away from is a common negative policy description in news.

sentences.en: Will the strained ceasefire last long enough for the U.S. to deliver that long-promised deal with Iran?
sentences.cn: Will the strained ceasefire last long enough for the U.S. to deliver on its long-promised deal with Iran?
sentences.structure: General question: Will + subject (the strained ceasefire) + predicate (last) + adverbial (long enough) + infinitive phrase (for the U.S. to deliver...).
sentences.analysis: strained ceasefire means "a ceasefire that is under tension"; long-promised is a compound adjective meaning "promised for a long time".

sentences.en: What will Bill Gates tell lawmakers about his links to the late convicted sex offender.
sentences.cn: What will Bill Gates tell lawmakers about his links to the late convicted sex offender?
sentences.structure: Special question: What + auxiliary (will) + subject (Bill Gates) + predicate (tell) + indirect object (lawmakers) + prepositional phrase (about his links...).
sentences.analysis: the late convicted sex offender means "the deceased convicted sex offender"; late means "deceased", convicted is a past participle used as an adjective.

sentences.en: Do you think that you've killed people as an adult?
sentences.cn: Do you think you have killed someone as an adult?
sentences.structure: General question (Do you think) + object clause (that you've killed people as an adult).
sentences.analysis: "as an adult" means "as an adult," contrasting with "as a child" later.

sentences.en: OpenAI and Anthropic plan to have IPOs at release before the end of this year, signaling to me that the A.I. bubble is about to pop.
sentences.cn: OpenAI and Anthropic plan to have IPOs by the end of this year, which signals to me that the A.I. bubble is about to pop.
sentences.structure: Main clause (OpenAI and Anthropic plan to have IPOs...) + present participle phrase (signaling to me that...) as an adverbial of result, containing an object clause (that the A.I. bubble is about to pop).
sentences.analysis: IPO stands for Initial Public Offering; "signal to sb. that..." means "indicate to someone that..."; "be about to pop" means "be about to burst."

sentences.en: To any of those who feel let down or disappointed or disillusioned, it is my job to earn your trust, faith, and support.
sentences.cn: To anyone who feels let down, frustrated, or disillusioned, it is my job to earn your trust, belief, and support.
sentences.structure: Prepositional phrase (To any of those...) as an adverbial, containing an attributive clause (who feel let down or disappointed or disillusioned); main clause (it is my job to earn...).
sentences.analysis: "let down" means "disappointed"; "disillusioned" means "disappointed in someone or something that you believed was good"; "it is my job to do sth." is a common emphatic structure.

sentences.en: And I will spend every day of this campaign.
sentences.cn: I will spend every day of this campaign.
sentences.structure: Simple sentence: subject (I) + predicate (will spend) + object (every day of this campaign).
sentences.analysis: "spend" is followed by a time noun, meaning "use time."

sentences.en: And if I have the privilege every day in the United States Senate doing exactly that.
sentences.cn: If I am fortunate enough to do the same every day in the United States Senate.
sentences.structure: Adverbial clause of condition (if I have the privilege...), containing a present participle phrase (doing exactly that) as an accompanying adverbial.
sentences.analysis: have the privilege doing sth. means 'to be honored to do sth.'; the United States Senate is a proper noun.

sentences.en: Graham Platner punches his ticket.
sentences.cn: Graham Platner has secured the nomination.
sentences.structure: Simple sentence: subject (Graham Platner) + predicate (punches) + object (his ticket).
sentences.analysis: punch one's ticket is an idiom meaning 'to secure (political) nomination or qualification'.

sentences.en: But now he's got to convince voters to choose him over a five-term senator.
sentences.cn: But now he has to convince voters to choose him over a five-term senator.
sentences.structure: Simple sentence: subject (he) + predicate ('s got to convince) + object (voters) + infinitive phrase (to choose him over...).
sentences.analysis: convince sb. to do sth. means 'to persuade someone to do something'; choose A over B means 'to choose A instead of B'; five-term is a compound adjective meaning 'serving five terms'.

sentences.en: Democrat Graham Platner, despite all the scandals, earned about three-fourths of primary voters' support over Governor Janet Mills.
sentences.cn: Democrat Graham Platner, despite all the scandals, won the support of about three-fourths of primary voters, surpassing Governor Janet Mills.
sentences.structure: Subject (Democrat Graham Platner) + parenthetical (despite all the scandals) + predicate (earned) + object (about three-fourths of primary voters' support) + adverbial (over Governor Janet Mills).
sentences.analysis: despite indicates concession; three-fourths is a fraction expression; over here means 'more than'.

sentences.en: Moments after clinching the nomination, he turned his attention to his new opponent, Republican Senator Susan Collins.
sentences.cn: Soon after securing the nomination, he turned his attention to his new opponent, Republican Senator Susan Collins.
sentences.structure: Time adverbial (Moments after clinching the nomination) + main clause (he turned his attention to...), where the object (his new opponent) is followed by an appositive (Republican Senator Susan Collins).
sentences.analysis: "clinch the nomination" means "to secure the nomination"; "turn one's attention to" means "to focus on".

sentences.en: Susan Collins may have started her career decades ago in Washington with good intentions, but she has become just as spineless and corrupt as the establishment she now serves.
sentences.cn: Susan Collins may have started her career in Washington with good intentions decades ago, but she has become as spineless and corrupt as the establishment she now serves.
sentences.structure: Compound sentence: first clause (Susan Collins may have started...) + but connects second clause (she has become...), which includes a comparative structure (as...as...) and a relative clause (she now serves).
sentences.analysis: "may have started" indicates a past speculation; "spineless" means "lacking courage"; "the establishment" refers to "the existing power structure".

sentences.en: If you are an independent voice, why do you vote with Donald Trump 95 percent of the time?
sentences.cn: If you are an independent voice, why do you vote with Donald Trump 95% of the time?
sentences.structure: Conditional adverbial clause (If you are...) + main clause (why do you vote...).
sentences.analysis: "independent voice" means "a person who thinks independently"; "vote with sb." means "to cast the same vote as someone".

sentences.en: Platner has a lot of work to do to get Mills voters on his side.
sentences.cn: Platner has a lot of work to do to get Mills voters on his side.
sentences.structure: Simple sentence: subject (Platner) + verb (has) + object (a lot of work) + infinitive phrase (to do to get...) acting as attribute and adverbial of purpose.
sentences.analysis: "get sb. on one's side" means "to win someone's support".

sentences.en: I couldn't honestly support Platner, even though some of the things that he says are really good.
sentences.cn: I cannot honestly support Platner, although some things he says are really good.
sentences.structure: Main clause (I couldn't honestly support Platner) + concessive clause (even though some of the things...), which contains a relative clause (that he says).
sentences.analysis: honestly is an adverb modifying support; even though introduces a concessive clause.

sentences.en: I don't think he's ready to be a senator.
sentences.cn: I don't think he's ready to be a senator.
sentences.structure: Main clause (I don't think) + object clause (he's ready to be a senator).
sentences.analysis: Negative transfer: the main clause is negative, the subordinate clause is affirmative.

sentences.en: I'm leaning towards Collins unless the guy shows something else, but I just -- he's not there yet.
sentences.cn: I'm leaning towards Collins unless the guy shows something else, but I just -- he's not there yet.
sentences.structure: Compound sentence: first clause (I'm leaning towards Collins unless...) + but connects second clause (I just -- he's not there yet).
sentences.analysis: lean towards means 'tend to support'; unless introduces a conditional clause; he's not there yet is an informal expression meaning 'he hasn't reached the standard'.

sentences.en: And so, what will you do in November if its Platner versus Collins?
sentences.cn: So, if it's Platner versus Collins in November, what will you do?
sentences.structure: Special question (what will you do...) + conditional clause (if it's Platner versus Collins).
sentences.analysis: its should be it's (typo); versus means 'against'.

sentences.en: I'm a Democrat, but I voted for Susan Collins several times, because I think that she does care about the state of Maine.
sentences.cn: I'm a Democrat, but I've voted for Susan Collins many times because I think she really cares about Maine.
sentences.structure: Compound sentence: first clause (I'm a Democrat) + but connects second clause (I voted for Susan Collins several times), followed by an adverbial clause of reason (because I think...), which contains an object clause (that she does care...).
sentences.analysis: does indicates emphasis; care about means "to be concerned about."

sentences.en: But it's going to be a real problem for me in November.
sentences.cn: But that's going to be a real problem for me in November.
sentences.structure: Simple sentence: subject (it) + predicate ('s going to be) + predicative (a real problem) + adverbial (for me in November).
sentences.analysis: be going to indicates future; a real problem means "a genuine issue."

sentences.en: And Max, I'm actually going to start with you, because you're with VoteVets, which means you guys had a dog in the fight in this race.
sentences.cn: Max, I'm actually going to start with you, because you're with VoteVets, which means you guys have a dog in this fight.
sentences.structure: Main clause (I'm actually going to start with you) + adverbial clause of reason (because you're with VoteVets), followed by a non-restrictive relative clause (which means...), which contains an object clause (you guys had a dog in the fight).
sentences.analysis: have a dog in the fight is an idiom meaning "to have a personal stake in the outcome."

sentences.en: Democrats' chances of flipping the Maine Senate seat.
sentences.cn: Democrats' chances of flipping the Maine Senate seat.
sentences.structure: Noun phrase: subject (Democrats' chances) + prepositional phrase (of flipping the Maine Senate seat) as modifier.
sentences.analysis: flip the seat means "to change the party control of the seat."

sentences.en: Is this race as close as you all hoped it would be, now that Graham has struggled with these scandals?
sentences.cn: Since Graham is struggling with these scandals, is this race as close as you all hoped?
sentences.structure: General question (Is this race as close as...) + comparative structure (as...as...) + object clause (you all hoped it would be) + adverbial clause of reason (now that...).
sentences.analysis: as close as means 'as close as'; now that means 'since'.

sentences.en: The race is always going to be close.
sentences.cn: This race is always going to be close.
sentences.structure: Simple sentence: subject (The race) + predicate (is always going to be) + predicative (close).
sentences.analysis: be going to indicates future; close here means 'neck and neck'.

sentences.en: Not with a five-term senator, it's not.
sentences.cn: Not against a five-term senator, it's not.
sentences.structure: Elliptical sentence: the full form is 'It is not close with a five-term senator.'
sentences.analysis: five-term is a compound adjective; it's not is an elliptical expression.

sentences.en: What I would say about the Susan Collins race is that we've seen in the past, is you're not going to win this seat by electing a Susan Collins light or putting forward a Susan Collins light in a race.
sentences.cn: What I would say about Susan Collins' campaign is that what we've seen in the past is you're not going to win this seat by electing a 'Susan Collins light' or putting forward a 'Susan Collins light' in the race.
sentences.structure: Subject clause (What I would say...) + linking verb (is) + predicative clause (that we've seen...), where the predicative clause contains another predicative clause (is you're not going to win...).
sentences.analysis: Susan Collins light means 'a watered-down version of Susan Collins', light indicates 'lightweight'; put forward means 'propose'.

sentences.en: You are going to beat her by energizing your base, particularly in a midterm election.
sentences.cn: You will beat her by energizing your base, especially in a midterm election.
sentences.structure: Simple sentence: Subject (You) + Predicate (are going to beat) + Object (her) + Adverbial of manner (by energizing your base) + Parenthetical (particularly in a midterm election).
sentences.analysis: "energize your base" means "motivate your core supporters"; "midterm election" refers to "elections held in the middle of a presidential term."

topics.title: Maine Senate Race: Platner vs. Collins
topics.content: In the Maine Democratic primary, scandal-ridden Graham Platner defeated Governor Janet Mills with about 75% of the vote and will challenge five-term Republican Senator Susan Collins. Platner needs to win over Mills' supporters, while Collins still has an advantage among independent voters. Democrats hope to flip the seat, but Platner's character issues may affect the race.

topics.title: AI Company IPOs and Bubble Concerns
topics.content: OpenAI and Anthropic plan to hold initial public offerings (IPOs) by the end of the year, raising concerns that the A.I. bubble is about to burst. Investors are questioning whether AI industry valuations are too high and whether the IPOs will mark a turning point for the sector.
topics.keywords: IPO · OpenAI · Anthropic · A.I. bubble · pop

topics.title: Colorado Governor Candidate Controversial Remarks
topics.content: Colorado Republican gubernatorial candidate Victor Marx claimed he killed someone as a child and asked, "Does it matter?", sparking public outcry. He is the frontrunner in the Republican primary, and his extreme remarks could affect the election.
topics.keywords: Victor Marx · gubernatorial · frontrunner · controversial · killed someone

quiz.question: In 'Democrats in Maine vote to stick with Graham Platner', what is the closest meaning of 'stick with'?
quiz.explanation: 'stick with' is a phrasal verb meaning 'to continue to support or choose'. Here it means Democrats decided to continue supporting Platner. Other options are literal or opposite meanings.

quiz.question: In 'the A.I. bubble is about to pop', what is the meaning of 'pop'?
quiz.explanation: 'pop' here means 'burst', referring to a bubble bursting. In economic contexts, 'bubble pops' is commonly used to indicate the collapse of a bubble.

quiz.question: In 'Graham Platner punches his ticket', what does 'punches his ticket' mean?
quiz.explanation: 'punch one's ticket' is an idiom meaning 'to secure a nomination or qualification', especially in a political context indicating successful advancement.

quiz.question: According to the text, after Platner wins the primary, what is the main challenge he faces?
quiz.explanation: The text mentions that Platner needs to 'get Mills voters on his side', i.e., win over Mills supporters, because Mills lost the primary and his voters might turn to Collins.

quiz.question: Which of the following statements about Susan Collins is correct?
options: A. She has a close relationship with Trump
B. She is a five-term senator
D. She is from the Democratic Party
quiz.explanation: The text describes Collins as a "five-term senator." She does not have a close relationship with Trump ('not a tight bond'), and she is a Republican.

quiz.question: What is the viewpoint cited in the text regarding AI IPOs?
options: A. The AI bubble is about to burst
B. AI companies are reasonably valued
C. IPO will drive AI development
D. The market is confident in AI
quiz.explanation: The viewpoint cited in the text is "the A.I. bubble is about to pop," suggesting that the IPO may be a signal of the bubble's peak.
